content:
# Exodus Chapter 35 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 106–107; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 78–79 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_035_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_035_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_035_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_035_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### Sabbath Command Reaffirmed (vv. 1–3)

**1** Moses assembled the entire community of the Sons of Israel and said to them, "These are the things that YHWH has commanded you to do: **2** Work may be done for six days, but the seventh day must be sacred to you — a sabbath of complete rest, devoted to YHWH. Anyone who does any work on it must be put to death. **3** You must not even light a fire in any of your dwellings on the sabbath day."

---

### A Call for Willing Offerings (vv. 4–9)

**4** Moses said to the entire community of the Sons of Israel, "This is what YHWH has commanded: **5** Take up a contribution from among yourselves for YHWH. Everyone whose heart moves them to give should bring YHWH's contribution — gold, silver, and bronze; **6** blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, along with fine linen; goat hair; **7** ram skins dyed red, fine leather, and acacia wood; **8** oil for the lamps, spices for the anointing oil and for the fragrant incense; **9** onyx stones and stones to be set in the ephod and the breastpiece.

---

### A Call for Skilled Workers (vv. 10–19)

**10** "And let every skilled person among you come and make everything YHWH has commanded: **11** the tabernacle itself — its tent, its covering, its clasps, its frames, its crossbars, its posts, and its bases; **12** the ark with its poles, the mercy seat, and the curtain that shields it; **13** the table with its poles and all its utensils, and the bread of the Presence; **14** the lampstand for light with its utensils and its lamps, and the oil for the light; **15** the altar of incense with its poles, the anointing oil and the fragrant incense, and the entrance screen for the doorway of the tabernacle; **16** the altar of burnt offering with its bronze grating, its poles, and all its utensils; the basin and its stand; **17** the hangings for the courtyard, its posts and their bases, and the screen for the gate of the courtyard; **18** the tent pegs for the tabernacle and the tent pegs for the courtyard, along with their cords; **19** the finely woven garments for service in the sanctuary — the sacred garments for Aaron the priest and the garments for his sons to serve as priests."

---

### The People Respond Wholeheartedly (vv. 20–29)

**20** Then the entire community of the Sons of Israel withdrew from Moses' presence. **21** And everyone whose heart stirred them and whose spirit moved them came forward and brought their contribution to YHWH for the work on the tent of meeting, for all its service, and for the sacred garments. **22** They came — both men and women — everyone whose heart was willing. They brought brooches, earrings, rings, and ornaments, every kind of gold jewelry. Everyone who could presented a wave offering of gold to YHWH. **23** Everyone who had blue, purple, or scarlet yarn, fine linen, goat hair, ram skins dyed red, or fine leather brought them. **24** Everyone who could make a contribution of silver or bronze brought it as YHWH's contribution, and everyone who had acacia wood for any part of the work brought it. **25** Every woman who was skilled with her hands spun with her own hands and brought what she had spun — the blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and the fine linen. **26** And all the women whose hearts moved them and who had the skill spun the goat hair. **27** The leaders brought the onyx stones and the stones to be set in the ephod and the breastpiece, **28** along with the spices and the oil for the light, for the anointing oil, and for the fragrant incense. **29** Every man and woman among the Sons of Israel whose heart moved them to contribute to any of the work that YHWH had commanded through Moses brought a freewill offering to YHWH.

---

### Bezalel and Oholiab — Filled with the Spirit (vv. 30–35)

**30** Then Moses said to the Sons of Israel, "Look — YHWH has called by name Bezalel son of Uri, son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah, **31** and he has filled him with the Spirit of God — with wisdom, with understanding, with knowledge, and with every kind of skill — **32** to design artistic works, to work in gold, silver, and bronze, **33** to cut and set stones, to carve wood, and to do every kind of artistic craft. **34** He has also given both him and Oholiab son of Ahisamach, of the tribe of Dan, the ability to teach others. **35** He has filled them with skill to do every kind of work — engraving, designing, embroidering in blue, purple, and scarlet yarn and fine linen, and weaving — doing every kind of craft and creating artistic designs.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Verse 1 — "Moses assembled"
The Hebrew וַיַּקְהֵל מֹשֶׁה (wayyaqhel Mosheh) gives this entire section its traditional name: Vayakhel ("and he assembled"). The verb is in the hiphil stem — Moses caused the community to gather. This is the first great assembly after the golden calf disaster (ch. 32) and the covenant renewal (ch. 34). The LXX in Vaticanus renders καὶ συνήθροισεν Μωυσῆς (and Moses gathered together). The dynamic force of the passage is restoration: the same community that broke the covenant now gathers to build the dwelling place of the God who has forgiven them. The instructions that follow in vv. 4–19 largely recapitulate the commands given in chapters 25–31, but now they are spoken to the whole people, not just Moses — and now the people must choose to respond.

### Verse 2 — Sabbath before sanctuary
The placement of the sabbath command before the tabernacle instructions is theologically significant. Even the sacred work of building YHWH's dwelling must yield to the sabbath. The Hebrew שַׁבַּת שַׁבָּתוֹן (shabbat shabbaton) is an intensive construction — literally "a sabbath of sabbath-rest" — indicating the most complete cessation imaginable. The LXX reads σάββατα ἀνάπαυσις ἁγία (sabbaths, a holy rest). The Mishnah (Shabbat 7:2) later derived its list of thirty-nine categories of prohibited work from the juxtaposition of sabbath law with tabernacle construction — reasoning that whatever was needed to build the tabernacle defines the "work" that sabbath prohibits.

### Verse 3 — "You must not even light a fire"
The Hebrew לֹא־תְבַעֲרוּ אֵשׁ (lo-teva'aru esh) specifically prohibits kindling fire on the sabbath. This is the only specific activity singled out from the general prohibition of work. The Karaites took this literally — no fire at all on the sabbath, sitting in darkness and cold. Rabbinic tradition interpreted it as prohibiting the initiation of fire, but permitting fire kindled before the sabbath to continue burning, thus allowing sabbath warmth and pre-prepared hot food. The LXX renders οὐ καύσετε πῦρ (you shall not burn fire). The dynamic rendering "you must not even light a fire" preserves the specific prohibition while "even" captures the emphatic specificity — this is not just heavy labor but any productive act, down to the striking of a flame.

### Verse 5 — "Whose heart moves them"
The Hebrew כֹּל נְדִיב לִבּוֹ (kol nediv libbo) — literally "every willing-one of his heart" — is the keynote of the entire chapter. The word נָדִיב means noble, generous, willing; it describes an internal impulse, not an external compulsion. YHWH commands the offering, but the offering must come from voluntary hearts. The LXX renders παρ᾽ ἑαυτοῦ κατὰ τὸ δόξαν τῇ ψυχῇ (from themselves, according to what seems good to the soul). This language of heart-willingness recurs with remarkable frequency throughout the chapter (vv. 5, 21, 22, 26, 29), creating a thematic drumbeat: what God most desires is not resources but willing hearts. After the golden calf — when the people eagerly donated their gold jewelry for an idol (32:2–3) — this voluntary giving for YHWH's tabernacle functions as an act of repentance and restoration.

### Verses 21–22 — The generosity of men and women together
The Hebrew is emphatic about the participation of both sexes: הָאֲנָשִׁים עַל־הַנָּשִׁים (ha-anashim al-hannashim, v. 22) — literally "the men upon/alongside the women." Some interpreters read this as "the men accompanied the women," suggesting the women led and the men followed. Others read it as "men in addition to women." Either way, the text insists that both men and women participated. The LXX reads παρὰ τῶν ἀνδρῶν (from the men), simplifying the construction. The gold jewelry mentioned — brooches (חָח), earrings (נֶזֶם), rings (טַבַּעַת), and ornaments (כוּמָז) — is notable because the same word נֶזֶם (earring) appeared in 32:2 when Aaron collected earrings for the golden calf. The gold that once built an idol now builds a sanctuary.

### Verses 25–26 — The women who spun
The skilled women (כָּל־אִשָּׁה חַכְמַת־לֵב, "every woman wise of heart") are highlighted for two distinct contributions. Those in v. 25 spun the dyed yarns and fine linen — standard textile work, though requiring great skill. Those in v. 26 spun the goat hair (עִזִּים), which required a rarer and more demanding technique — spinning the coarse hair of goats into fabric strong enough for tent coverings. The text singles them out as women "whose hearts lifted them" (נָשָׂא לִבָּן), using the same heart-language that characterizes the entire chapter. The LXX renders this as αἷς ἔδοξεν τῇ διανοίᾳ αὐτῶν (those to whom it seemed good in their mind). The dynamic rendering preserves the distinction between the two groups while conveying the voluntary nature of their contribution.

### Verse 27 — "The leaders brought"
The Hebrew וְהַנְּשִׂאִם הֵבִיאוּ (wehannesi'im hevi'u) — "and the leaders brought" — has an unusual spelling. The word נְּשִׂאִם (leaders) is missing a yod (י), written defectively as נשאם rather than the expected נשיאם. Rashi, following the midrash (Bamidbar Rabbah 12:16), interpreted this defective spelling as a subtle rebuke: the leaders said they would wait and contribute whatever the people did not supply, but when the people gave so abundantly that nearly everything was covered, the leaders had little left to bring. Their generosity was real but delayed, and the missing letter reflects their diminished initiative. The LXX in Vaticanus shows no trace of this wordplay. Whether or not one accepts the midrashic reading, the text positions the leaders' contribution — precious stones and spices — as a distinct category from the people's giving.

### Verses 30–31 — "Filled with the Spirit of God"
The Hebrew וַיְמַלֵּא אֹתוֹ רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים (waymalle' oto ruach Elohim) describes Bezalel as filled with the Spirit of God — the same language used in 31:3 when YHWH first announced his appointment. The word רוּחַ (ruach) means wind, breath, spirit — the animating force of God himself. What is striking is that this divine filling produces not prophecy or military power but artistic skill: חָכְמָה (wisdom), תְּבוּנָה (understanding), דַּעַת (knowledge), and כָּל־מְלָאכָה (every kind of craft). The LXX renders πνεῦμα θεῖον σοφίας καὶ συνέσεως (a divine spirit of wisdom and understanding). The theology is profound — the same Spirit that hovered over the waters at creation (Gen 1:2) now fills a craftsman to build a dwelling where heaven and earth meet. Artistic work is here presented not as mere human talent but as a form of divine inspiration.

### Verse 34 — "The ability to teach others"
The Hebrew וּלְהוֹרֹת נָתַן בְּלִבּוֹ (ulehorot natan belibbo) — literally "and to teach he put in his heart" — adds a dimension not mentioned in the original commission in chapter 31. Bezalel and Oholiab are not only master craftsmen but also teachers. The verb הוֹרָה (to teach, instruct) is from the same root as תּוֹרָה (Torah, instruction). The LXX renders καὶ προβιβάσαι ἔδωκεν αὐτῷ (and he gave him to advance/instruct). The gift of teaching ensures that the skills do not die with one generation. What God gives to individuals is meant to be transmitted — the Spirit's gifts are not for private hoarding but for building community capacity.

### Verse 35 — The catalog of crafts
The final verse catalogs the full range of skills given to Bezalel and Oholiab: חָרָשׁ (engraving), חֹשֵׁב (designing — literally "thinking," the work of artistic conception), רֹקֵם (embroidering — needlework in colored yarns), and אֹרֵג (weaving — loom work). The distinction between חֹשֵׁב and רֹקֵם is significant: the former describes figured textile work where the design appears on both sides of the fabric (used for the tabernacle curtains and the ephod), while the latter describes needlework where the design appears on one side only (used for the courtyard screen). The LXX renders these with ὑφαντικῆς (weaving) and ποικιλτικῆς (embroidery). The verse concludes with a comprehensive summary — כָּל־מְלָאכָה (every kind of work) and מַחֲשָׁבֹת (designs, plans) — placing artistic creativity firmly within the realm of divine gifting.

### Vaticanus Divergences
The LXX in Vaticanus shows several notable features in Exodus 35: (1) The chapter division and verse ordering differ significantly from the MT — the LXX places the tabernacle construction materials list (corresponding to MT vv. 11–19) in a different sequence, reflecting the broader phenomenon where LXX Exodus 35–40 follows a substantially different arrangement from the Hebrew. (2) In v. 21, Vaticanus reads ὧν ἔδοξεν τῇ ψυχῇ αὐτῶν (whose soul was pleased), interpreting the Hebrew "heart stirred" as a soul-level decision rather than an emotional impulse. (3) In v. 22, the LXX uses κόμβον (clasp/brooch) for the Hebrew חָח, suggesting the translators understood this as a fastening device rather than a nose-ring. (4) Vaticanus presents a considerably shorter text in the second half of the chapter compared to the MT, reflecting the well-known phenomenon that the LXX version of the tabernacle construction narrative (chs. 35–40) is significantly abbreviated relative to the Hebrew — omitting or condensing many of the detailed repetitions found in the MT. This is one of the most striking textual divergences between the Hebrew and Greek traditions in the entire Pentateuch. (5) The Bezalel passage in Vaticanus follows the MT closely in substance, including the reference to the divine spirit (πνεῦμα θεῖον) and the teaching commission, though with minor variations in word order and vocabulary.
